title: Contributing

title: Event and Accumulation Dates — event
text: implementation chronological modeling method proposed Bellanger Husi (2012, 2013). Event accumulation dates density estimates occupation duration archaeological site (Bellanger Husi 2012, 2013). event date estimation terminus post-quem archaeological assemblage. accumulation date represents "chronological profile" assemblage. According Bellanger Husi (2012), accumulation date can interpreted "best ... formation process reflecting duration succession events scale archaeological time, worst, imprecise dating due contamination context residual intrusive material." words, accumulation dates estimate occurrence archaeological events rhythms long term. method relies strong archaeological statistical assumptions. Use know (see references vignette: utils::vignette("dating")).

title: Paul Tol's Colour Schemes
text: general rule, ordered data represented using rainbow scheme. three main arguments use (Tol 2021): spectral order visible light carries inherent magnitude message. bands almost constant hue sharp transitions , can perceived jumps data. Colour-blind people difficulty distinguishing colours rainbow. use avoided, Paul Tol’s technical note provides two colour schemes reasonably clear colour-blind vision. remain colour-blind safe, two schemes must comply following conditions: discrete rainbow scheme must interpolated. smooth rainbow scheme used full range (Tol 2021 suggests starting purple).  using smooth rainbow scheme: Start -white instead purple lowest data value occurs often; End red instead brown highest data value occurs often.
code: discrete_rainbow <- colour("discrete rainbow") plot_scheme(discrete_rainbow(14), colours = TRUE, size = 0.7) smooth_rainbow <- colour("smooth rainbow")  ## Start at purple instead of off-white plot(smooth_rainbow(256, range = c(0.25, 1))) ## End at red instead of brown plot(smooth_rainbow(256, range = c(0, 0.9)))

title: Colour Schemes for Scientific Data Visualization
text: Color blindness affects large number individuals. communicating scientific results colour palettes must therefore carefully chosen accessible readers. R package provides implementation Okabe Ito[1], Paul Tol[2] Fabio Crameri[3] colour schemes. schemes ready type data (qualitative, diverging sequential), colours distinct people, including colour-blind readers. package also provides tools simulate colour-blindness test well colours palette identifiable. simulate colour-blindness production-ready R figures may also interested colorblindr package. specific uses, several scientific thematic schemes (geologic timescale, land cover, FAO soils, etc.) implemented, colour schemes may colour-blind safe. colour schemes implemented use base R ggplot2.

title: Manual
text: arkhe package provides set S4 classes archaeological data matrices. new classes represent different special types matrix. CountMatrix represents absolute frequency data, OccurrenceMatrix represents co-occurrence matrix, CompositionMatrix represents relative frequency data, IncidenceMatrix represents presence/absence data, StratigraphicMatrix represents stratigraphic relationships. assumes keep data tidy: variable (taxon/type) must saved column observation (assemblage/sample) must saved row. Note missing values allowed. internal structure S4 classes implemented arkhe depicted UML class diagram following figure. UML class diagram S4 classes structure.

title: Manual
text: denote \(m \times p\) count matrix \(= \left[ a_{ij} \right] ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) row column sums: \[\begin{align}  a_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} a_{ij} &&  a_{\cdot j} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} a_{ij} &&  a_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} \sum_{j = 1}^{p} a_{ij} &&  \forall a_{ij} \\mathbb{N} \end{align}\]

title: Manual
text: frequency matrix represents relative abundances. denote \(m \times p\) frequency matrix \(B = \left[ b_{ij} \right] ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) row column sums: \[\begin{align}  b_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} b_{ij} = 1 &&  b_{\cdot j} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} b_{ij} &&  b_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} \sum_{j = 1}^{p} b_{ij} &&  \forall b_{ij} \\left[ 0,1 \right] \end{align}\]

title: Manual
text: co-occurrence matrix symmetric matrix zeros main diagonal, works many times pairs taxa occur together least one sample. \(p \times p\) co-occurrence matrix \(D = \left[ d_{,j} \right] ~\forall ,j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) defined \(m \times p\) abundance matrix \(= \left[ a_{x,y} \right] ~\forall x \\left[ 1,m \right], y \\left[ 1,p \right]\) : \[ d_{,j} = \sum_{x = 1}^{m} \bigcap_{y = }^{j} a_{xy} \] row column sums: \[\begin{align}   d_{\cdot} = \sum_{j \geqslant }^{p} d_{ij} &&   d_{\cdot j} = \sum_{\leqslant j}^{p} d_{ij} &&   d_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{p} \sum_{j \geqslant }^{p} d_{ij} &&   \forall d_{ij} \\mathbb{N} \end{align}\]

title: Manual
text: denote \(m \times p\) incidence matrix \(C = \left[ c_{ij} \right] ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) row column sums: \[\begin{align}  c_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} c_{ij} &&  c_{\cdot j} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} c_{ij} &&  c_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} \sum_{j = 1}^{p} c_{ij} &&  \forall c_{ij} \\lbrace 0,1 \rbrace \end{align}\]

code: ### Create a count matrix A0 <- matrix(data = sample(0:10, 100, TRUE), nrow = 10, ncol = 10)  ### Coerce to absolute frequencies A1 <- as_count(A0)  ### Coerce to relative frequencies B <- as_composition(A1)  ### Row sums are internally stored before coercing to a frequency matrix ### (use get_totals() to get these values) ### This allows to restore the source data A2 <- as_count(B) all(A1 == A2) #> [1] TRUE  ### Coerce to presence/absence C <- as_incidence(A1)  ### Coerce to a co-occurrence matrix D <- as_occurrence(A1)

title: Representation of Archaeological Data
text: collection classes represent archaeological data. package provides set S4 classes represent different special types matrix (absolute/relative frequency, presence/absence data, co-occurrence matrix, etc.) upon package developers can build subclasses. also provides set generic methods (mutators coercion mechanisms) functions (e.g. predicates). addition, classes general interest (e.g. represent stratigraphic relationships) implemented.

title: Representation of Archaeological Data
text: arkhe provides set S4 classes represent different special types matrix. CountMatrix represents absolute frequency data, OccurrenceMatrix represents co-occurrence matrix, CompositionMatrix represents relative frequency (compositional) data, IncidenceMatrix represents presence/absence data, StratigraphicMatrix represents stratigraphic relationships. assumes keep data tidy: variable (type/taxa) must saved column observation (assemblage/sample) must saved row. new classes simple use inherit base matrix: arkhe uses coercing mechanisms (validation methods) data type conversions: CountMatrix, CompositionMatrix IncidenceMatrix classes special slots: samples replicated measurements/observation, groups group data site/area. tqp taq specify chronology assemblage. way chronological information handled somewhat opinionated: Sub-annual precision overkill/meaningless situations: dates assumed expressed years CE stored integers (values coerced .integer() hence truncated towards zero). date range (.e. TPQ/TAQ) expected, although possible specify point estimate. coercing data.frame *Matrix object, attempt made automatically assign values slots. behavior can disabled setting options(arkhe.autodetect = FALSE).
code: ## Load the package library(arkhe) ## Define a count data matrix ## (data will be rounded to zero decimal places, then coerced with as.integer) quanti <- CountMatrix(data = sample(0:10, 100, TRUE), nrow = 10, ncol = 10)  ## Define a logical matrix ## (data will be coerced with as.logical) quali <- IncidenceMatrix(data = sample(0:1, 100, TRUE), nrow = 10, ncol = 10) ## Create a count matrix X <- matrix(data = sample(0:10, 75, TRUE), nrow = 15, ncol = 5)  ## Coerce to absolute frequencies A1 <- as_count(X)  ## Coerce to relative frequencies B <- as_composition(A1)  ## Row sums are internally stored before coercing to a frequency matrix ## (use get_totals() to get these values) ## This allows to restore the source data A2 <- as_count(B) all(A1 == A2) #> [1] TRUE  ## Coerce to presence/absence D <- as_incidence(A1)  ## Coerce to a co-occurrence matrix E <- as_occurrence(A1) Y <- as.data.frame(X) Y$samples <- rep(c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e"), each = 3) Y$groups <- rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 5) Y$tpq <- sample(1301:1400, 15, TRUE) # TPQ Y$taq <- sample(1451:1500, 15, TRUE) # TAQ  ## Coerce to a count matrix Z <- as_count(Y)  ## Get groups get_samples(Z) #>  [1] "a" "a" "a" "b" "b" "b" "c" "c" "c" "d" "d" "d" "e" "e" "e"  ## Get groups get_groups(Z) #>  [1] "A" "A" "A" "A" "A" "B" "B" "B" "B" "B" "C" "C" "C" "C" "C"  ## Get dates get_dates(Z) #>     tpq  taq #> 1  1347 1455 #> 2  1360 1490 #> 3  1387 1453 #> 4  1315 1463 #> 5  1388 1482 #> 6  1392 1476 #> 7  1379 1471 #> 8  1344 1464 #> 9  1347 1454 #> 10 1333 1490 #> 11 1387 1473 #> 12 1318 1463 #> 13 1382 1462 #> 14 1364 1461 #> 15 1303 1491
